topic:
A Methodological Framework for Orthodontic Treatment Outcome Prediction:
    How to Leverage Multimodal Deep Learning with 2D Intra-oral Photographs
    and 3D Intra-oral Scans.
    This paper addresses a novel clinical problem with limited prior art:
    given (1) a longitudinal dataset of 2D intra-oral photographs (maxillary
    occlusal, mandibular occlusal, frontal, left buccal, right buccal views)
    taken at periodic checkpoints throughout completed orthodontic treatments,
    and (2) paired pre- and post-treatment 3D intra-oral scan datasets, build
    a system that takes a new patient's baseline 2D photographs and 3D scan as
    input and outputs predicted intermediate treatment-progress images and the
    final post-treatment outcome image.
    Because direct prior work on this exact problem is scarce, the paper's
    central contribution is a technical deep-dive into which existing techniques
    from adjacent fields are most applicable, how each technique works, why it
    is suitable for this problem, and how they should be composed into a
    coherent pipeline. Specifically, the paper should explain and justify
    choices across: (a) 3D dental data representation — point clouds,
    mesh-based methods, implicit neural representations (NeRF, SDF), and
    voxel grids; (b) 2D-3D multimodal fusion strategies — cross-attention,
    projection-based alignment, and contrastive cross-modal pretraining;
    (c) generative models for image synthesis — conditional diffusion models,
    GANs (pix2pix, CycleGAN), and neural rendering; (d) temporal tooth
    movement modeling — video diffusion, latent space interpolation, and
    physics-informed neural networks; (e) training strategies given limited
    clinical data — transfer learning, data augmentation, self-supervised
    pretraining, and synthetic data generation. The paper should propose a
    concrete recommended pipeline architecture and discuss why each component
    was chosen over alternatives. No real experiments are conducted — this is
    a methodological proposal grounded in technical reasoning and adjacent
    literature.
